formulas.time: T = [v₀ sin θ + √((v₀ sin θ)² + 2gh₀)] / g
formulas.title: Formulas

errors.flat: No projectile at this angle, increase angle.

T.approx: The flight time of both projectiles is nearly equal (≈ {val} s). The vertical velocity components and initial heights are almost the same, resulting in similar values in the T = (v₀sinθ + √…)/g formula.
T.p1Higher: Projectile 1 stays in the air longer (T₁ = {t1} s > T₂ = {t2} s). Reason: {reasons}. Objects stay in the air longer with higher vertical velocity or height.
T.p2Higher: Projectile 2 stays in the air longer (T₂ = {t2} s > T₁ = {t1} s). Reason: {reasons}. Objects stay in the air longer with higher vertical velocity or height.

H.approx: Both projectiles reached nearly the same height (≈ {val} m). Since vertical velocity and initial height are equal, the height H in H = h₀ + (v₀sinθ)²/2g remains the same.
H.p1Higher: Projectile 1 reached a greater height (H₁ = {h1} m > H₂ = {h2} m). Reason: {reasons}. In H = h₀ + (v₀sinθ)²/2g, height increases with the square of the vertical velocity.
H.p2Higher: Projectile 2 reached a greater height (H₂ = {h2} m > H₁ = {h1} m). Reason: {reasons}. In H = h₀ + (v₀sinθ)²/2g, height increases with the square of the vertical velocity.

R.complementary: Both projectiles have the same range (≈ {val} m), a famous symmetry in projectile motion! Complementary angles {theta1}° and {theta2}° (summing to 90°) yield the same range at the same initial velocity because R = v₀²sin(2θ)/g and sin(2θ) = sin(180°−2θ).
R.approx: Both projectiles have nearly the same range (≈ {val} m). This occurs because the product of horizontal velocity and flight time (R = v₀cosθ × T) is very close.
R.p1Higher: Projectile 1 travels further (R₁ = {r1} m > R₂ = {r2} m). Reason: {reasons}. In R = v₀cosθ × T, increasing both factors increases the range.
R.p2Higher: Projectile 2 travels further (R₂ = {r2} m > R₁ = {r1} m). Reason: {reasons}. In R = v₀cosθ × T, increasing both factors increases the range.
